short_description: Straight-line distance between two points; the square root of the sum of squared coordinate differences.
assumptions: p and q are real vectors of equal length
when_to_use: Default geometric distance between points in R^n when all coordinates share a scale.
when_not_to_use: When features have very different scales or strong correlations (use Mahalanobis), or when comparing probability distributions.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: For high dimensions prefer vectorized norms (np.linalg.norm).

short_description: Sum of absolute coordinate differences; the L1 norm of the difference vector.
assumptions: p and q are real vectors of equal length
when_to_use: When movement is axis-aligned, or when you want robustness to outliers relative to L2.
when_not_to_use: When diagonal/straight-line geometry matters.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Underlies L1 regularization (LASSO).

short_description: Tunable family of vector distances; the parameter k unifies L1, L2 and L-infinity.
assumptions: k >= 1 for a true metric (the triangle inequality fails for 0 < k < 1)
practical_use_cases: tunable distance for k-NN and clustering
when_to_use: When you want a single dial between L1 (robust) and L-infinity (worst coordinate).
when_not_to_use: With 0<k<1, where it is no longer a metric.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Use np.linalg.norm(diff, ord=k).

short_description: The largest absolute coordinate difference; the limit of Minkowski as k -> infinity.
assumptions: p and q are real vectors of equal length
when_to_use: When the worst single-coordinate deviation is what matters.
when_not_to_use: When all coordinates should contribute jointly.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: np.max(np.abs(p-q)).

short_description: A feature-weighted distance defined by a PSD matrix Q; Mahalanobis is the special case Q = Sigma^{-1}.
formula_latex: d_Q(\mathbf p,\mathbf q)=\sqrt{(\mathbf p-\mathbf q)^{\top} Q\,(\mathbf p-\mathbf q)}\,,\quad Q \succeq 0
assumptions: Q is symmetric positive semidefinite (positive definite for a true metric)

parameters.name: Q
parameters.constraints: symmetric PSD; PD for a metric

when_to_use: When some directions/features should count more, or cross-feature terms matter.
when_not_to_use: When Q is unknown or hard to estimate; fall back to Euclidean.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: With only PSD (not PD) Q this is a pseudometric (distinct points can have zero distance).

short_description: Scale- and correlation-aware distance using the inverse covariance; equals Euclidean distance after whitening.
formula_latex: d_\Sigma(\mathbf p,\mathbf q)=\sqrt{(\mathbf p-\mathbf q)^{\top} \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf p-\mathbf q)}
assumptions: Sigma is a symmetric positive-definite covariance matrix
when_to_use: When features are correlated and on different scales.
when_not_to_use: When Sigma cannot be reliably estimated (high dimension, few samples).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Solve against Sigma rather than forming Sigma^{-1} explicitly for stability.

short_description: Number of positions at which two equal-length sequences differ.
assumptions: sequences have equal length
when_to_use: Categorical or binary sequences of equal length.
when_not_to_use: Sequences of different length or where insertions/deletions matter (use Levenshtein).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: np.sum(a != b).
worked_example: $"10110"$ vs $"10011"$ differ in positions 3 and 4 $\Rightarrow d_H=\mathbf{2}$.

short_description: Minimum number of single-character insertions, deletions, or substitutions to turn one string into another.
formula_plaintext: min number of insert/delete/substitute edits from a to b
assumptions: unit cost per edit (weighted variants exist)
when_to_use: Comparing strings of possibly different length where edits matter.
when_not_to_use: Equal-length symbol streams with no insertions/deletions (Hamming is cheaper).
special_cases: Reduces toward Hamming when only substitutions are allowed and lengths are equal

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Classic O(mn) dynamic-programming table.

short_description: Alignment-based dissimilarity that warps the time axis to match two sequences; not a true metric.
formula_plaintext: min over warping paths pi of sum of local costs d(a_i, b_j)
assumptions: a monotone, continuous warping path with boundary conditions
when_to_use: Sequences that are similar in shape but shifted/stretched in time.
when_not_to_use: When you need the triangle inequality (DTW violates it).
special_cases: Reduces to Euclidean alignment when no warping is allowed

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: O(mn) DP over an accumulated cost matrix; add Sakoe-Chiba band for speed.

short_description: Length of the shortest path between two points on a curved manifold, set by the metric tensor g.
formula_plaintext: L(gamma) = integral sqrt(g_ij xdot^i xdot^j) dt, minimized over paths
assumptions: a Riemannian metric tensor g(x) on the manifold
when_to_use: When data lives on a known curved space and intrinsic distance matters.
when_not_to_use: When no metric tensor / manifold structure is available.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: No closed form for general g; solve the geodesic ODE numerically (e.g. with geomstats).
worked_example: On a unit sphere, two points at angular separation $\theta$ have geodesic length $L=\theta$ (orthogonal points $\Rightarrow \pi/2\approx\mathbf{1.571}$).

short_description: The natural Riemannian metric tensor on a statistical model; the local curvature of log-likelihood. A metric tensor, not a distance.
formula_plaintext: I(theta) = E[ (grad_theta ln p)(grad_theta ln p)^T ]
assumptions: a smooth parametric family p(x|theta) with regularity conditions
when_to_use: When you need the local metric on a parametric model (for natural gradient or efficiency bounds).
when_not_to_use: As a distance between two distributions directly (integrate it into Fisher-Rao instead).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: This is a matrix (metric tensor), not a scalar distance.

short_description: Geodesic distance between distributions on a statistical manifold whose metric is the Fisher information.
formula_plaintext: rho_FR(p,q) = min over paths of integral sqrt(gammadot^T I gammadot) dt
assumptions: distributions belong to a smooth parametric family
when_to_use: When you want the principled, parametrization-invariant distance between models.
when_not_to_use: When no closed form exists and approximations suffice (KL is often easier).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Closed form only for special families (e.g. Gaussians); otherwise integrate the geodesic.

short_description: Master family of divergences generated by a convex function f with f(1)=0; KL, chi-square, Hellinger and TV are special cases.
formula_plaintext: Df(p||q) = integral p f(q/p) dmu, f convex, f(1)=0
assumptions: f convex with f(1)=0
p, q are distributions on the same space
when_to_use: When you want one framework that specializes to many named divergences.
when_not_to_use: Directly — pick a concrete generator f.

references.title: Nielsen & Nock, On the chi-square and higher-order divergences
Taxonomy (this site)

implementation_notes: Only total variation among f-divergences is itself a metric.
worked_example: Plug in a generator f to recover a specific divergence; see KL, Hellinger, TV entries for numbers.

short_description: Expected log-ratio of p to q; the canonical asymmetric divergence and the only one that is both an f-divergence and a Bregman divergence.
assumptions: q(x)=0 implies p(x)=0 (absolute continuity), else KL is infinite
same support / base measure
when_to_use: When you compare a model q to data/reference p and direction (from/to) is meaningful.
when_not_to_use: When you need symmetry, boundedness, or a metric, or when supports differ.
special_cases: Bregman divergence with F = -H (negative Shannon entropy)

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Clip probabilities with eps for stability
Renormalize inputs to sum to 1

short_description: KL with arguments swapped, KL(q||p); minimizing it is mode-seeking (zero-forcing).
assumptions: p(x)=0 implies q(x)=0 for finiteness
when_to_use: When you want the approximation to concentrate on (a) mode(s) of the target.
when_not_to_use: When mass-covering behaviour is desired (use forward KL).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Just KL with arguments swapped; same numerical safeguards.

short_description: An f-divergence that locally approximates KL to second order; basis of the chi-square goodness-of-fit test.
assumptions: p_i > 0 wherever evaluated
when_to_use: Goodness-of-fit and as a tractable surrogate near agreement.
when_not_to_use: When tails differ a lot (chi-square is tail-sensitive) or symmetry is needed.
special_cases: f-divergence with f(t)=(t-1)^2; Pearson-Vajda chi^k with k=2

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Sensitive to small p_i; clip denominators.

short_description: The Pearson chi-square with the roles of p and q swapped.
assumptions: q_i > 0 wherever evaluated
when_to_use: When the reference is q and you want a chi-square against it.
when_not_to_use: When symmetry is required.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Clip q to avoid division by tiny values.

short_description: A symmetric, bounded, true metric between distributions; the L2 distance between square-root densities.
assumptions: p, q are distributions on the same space
when_to_use: When you want a bounded, symmetric, genuine metric between probability distributions.
when_not_to_use: When ground-distance/optimal-transport geometry matters.
special_cases: f-divergence with f(t)=(sqrt t - 1)^2; related to Bhattacharyya coefficient

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Numerically stable (no logs); just renormalize inputs.

identities.note: half the L1 distance = largest event gap

short_description: Half the L1 distance between distributions; the largest possible difference in probability assigned to any event. The only f-divergence that is a metric.
assumptions: p, q are distributions on the same space
when_to_use: When you want the strongest natural notion of distinguishability with a metric and a clear bound.
when_not_to_use: When you need sensitivity to ground distances between outcomes.
special_cases: f-divergence with f(t)=|t-1|/2; also an IPM over functions bounded by 1

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Pinsker: TV <= sqrt(KL/2).

short_description: A one-parameter f-divergence family that interpolates KL, reverse-KL and Hellinger; central to information geometry.
assumptions: p, q are distributions; -1 < alpha < 1 for the displayed form
when_to_use: When you want to dial between mass-covering and mode-seeking behaviour.
when_not_to_use: When a single fixed, interpretable divergence suffices.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: The alpha=+-1 cases are limits computed as KL.

short_description: Master family generated by a strictly convex potential F: the gap between F and its tangent plane.
formula_plaintext: B_F(x||y) = F(x) - F(y) - <x - y, grad F(y)>
assumptions: F strictly convex and differentiable
when_to_use: When you want a divergence whose mean minimizer is the arithmetic mean (Bregman centroid).
when_not_to_use: Directly — choose a concrete potential F.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Generally asymmetric and not a metric (squared-Euclidean is the symmetric exception).
worked_example: Pick F to recover a member; see Squared Euclidean, Itakura-Saito, Log-Det entries.

short_description: The Bregman divergence of F(x)=||x||^2; equals the squared Euclidean distance. Not a metric, but its square root is.
assumptions: real vectors
when_to_use: Default Bregman divergence; gives arithmetic-mean centroids in k-means.
when_not_to_use: When you need the triangle inequality (use Euclidean distance = its square root).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: np.sum((x-y)**2).

short_description: Bregman divergence of F(x)=sum x log x on the positive orthant; KL for unnormalized nonnegative vectors.
formula_plaintext: B_F(x||y) = sum_i ( x_i log(x_i / y_i) - x_i + y_i )
assumptions: x_i, y_i > 0
when_to_use: Comparing nonnegative (possibly unnormalized) vectors such as counts or intensities.
when_not_to_use: When inputs are not nonnegative.
special_cases: Reduces to KL on normalized probability vectors

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Clip with eps for the logs.
worked_example: Equals KL on normalized inputs; for the running $p,q$: $\approx\mathbf{0.511}$.

short_description: Scale-invariant Bregman divergence from F(x)=-sum log x; standard for spectral/audio data and NMF.
assumptions: p_i, q_i > 0
when_to_use: Comparing power spectra where relative (scale-invariant) error matters.
when_not_to_use: When inputs can be zero/negative.
special_cases: Bregman divergence with F(x)=-sum log x

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Scale-invariant: IS(c p || c q) = IS(p||q).

identities.note: Bregman divergence of the Burg entropy

identities.latex: \text{von Neumann divergence is the Bregman divergence of }-S(\rho)

short_description: Bregman divergence on SPD matrices from F(X)=-log det X; compares covariance/kernel matrices.
formula_latex: D(\mathbf P\|\mathbf Q)=\langle\mathbf P,\mathbf Q^{-1}\rangle-\log\det(\mathbf P\mathbf Q^{-1})-n
formula_plaintext: D(P||Q) = tr(P Q^{-1}) - log det(P Q^{-1}) - n
assumptions: P, Q are symmetric positive-definite n x n matrices
when_to_use: Comparing SPD matrices when you want a Bregman (information-theoretic) geometry.
when_not_to_use: When you need a symmetric metric on SPD (use affine-invariant Riemannian or Bures-Wasserstein).
special_cases: Matrix Bregman divergence with F(X)=-log det X (Burg entropy seed)

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Use np.linalg.solve, not an explicit inverse
Check slogdet sign and raise on non-SPD inputs

short_description: Euclidean distance between matrices, treating them as long vectors; the square root of the squared-Frobenius matrix Bregman divergence.
assumptions: X, Y are matrices of the same shape
when_to_use: Simple, fast baseline distance between matrices; ignores SPD geometry.
when_not_to_use: When the SPD manifold's curvature matters (use affine-invariant Riemannian or Bures-Wasserstein).
special_cases: Squared version is the matrix Bregman divergence with F=||X||_F^2

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: np.linalg.norm(X-Y, 'fro').

short_description: Matrix Bregman divergence of the von Neumann entropy seed; the quantum analogue of KL between density/SPD matrices.
formula_latex: D(\mathbf P\|\mathbf Q)=\mathrm{Tr}\big(\mathbf P(\log\mathbf P-\log\mathbf Q)-\mathbf P+\mathbf Q\big)
formula_plaintext: D(P||Q) = tr( P (log P - log Q) - P + Q )
assumptions: P, Q are SPD (density) matrices
when_to_use: An information-theoretic divergence between density matrices.
when_not_to_use: When a symmetric SPD metric is required.
special_cases: Reduces to KL on the eigenvalues for commuting (diagonal) P, Q

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Use eigendecomposition for matrix log; both inputs must be SPD.

short_description: Minus log of the Bhattacharyya coefficient (the overlap integral of sqrt(p) and sqrt(q)); a symmetric class-separability measure.
assumptions: p, q are distributions on the same space
when_to_use: Quick symmetric overlap-based separability score between distributions.
when_not_to_use: When you need the triangle inequality (use Hellinger, which is the metric cousin).
special_cases: Chernoff information at alpha=1/2; related to Hellinger via the Bhattacharyya coefficient

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: BC = sum sqrt(p q); distance = -log(BC).

short_description: The best achievable error exponent in binary hypothesis testing; an optimized alpha-power overlap.
formula_plaintext: C(p,q) = max_{alpha in (0,1)} ( -log sum_i p_i^alpha q_i^(1-alpha) )
assumptions: p, q are distributions on the same space
when_to_use: When you want the asymptotic error rate of the optimal test between two hypotheses.
when_not_to_use: When a simple closed-form divergence suffices.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Grid-search or optimize alpha in (0,1).

short_description: A one-parameter family of divergences from the alpha-power affinity; tends to KL as alpha -> 1.
assumptions: alpha > 0, alpha != 1
when_to_use: When you need a tunable divergence, especially for privacy composition.
when_not_to_use: When symmetry or a metric is required.
special_cases: alpha->1 KL; alpha=1/2 related to Bhattacharyya

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: At alpha=1 fall back to KL (the limit).

short_description: The sum of the two KL directions; symmetric but unbounded and not a metric.
assumptions: p, q mutually absolutely continuous for finiteness
practical_use_cases: symmetric KL when direction is arbitrary
when_to_use: When you want symmetry but are fine with unboundedness.
when_not_to_use: When you need a bounded score (use Jensen-Shannon).
special_cases: Sum of forward and reverse KL

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Just KL(p||q)+KL(q||p) with the usual eps clipping.

identities.note: mean of KLs to the mixture

inequalities.note: the Jensen–Shannon distance obeys the triangle inequality

short_description: Bounded, symmetric divergence: the average of the two KL divergences to the mixture m=(p+q)/2; its square root is a metric.
assumptions: p, q are distributions on the same space (always finite)
when_to_use: When you want a stable, bounded, symmetric divergence (and a metric via its square root).
when_not_to_use: When you need optimal-transport geometry over ground distances (use Wasserstein).
special_cases: Burbea-Rao divergence generated by negative Shannon entropy F=-H

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Clip probabilities with eps
Always finite even on disjoint supports (bounded by log 2)

short_description: The Jensen gap of a convex F evaluated at p, q and their midpoint; Jensen-Shannon is the negative-Shannon-entropy case.
assumptions: F convex
when_to_use: When you want a symmetric divergence from any convex generator.
when_not_to_use: Directly — pick a generator F.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: With F=-H it equals JS exactly.

short_description: The average information content (uncertainty) of a single distribution; the functional behind KL and Jensen-Shannon.
assumptions: p is a probability distribution
when_to_use: Quantifying uncertainty/spread of one distribution.
when_not_to_use: As a distance between two distributions (use KL or JS).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Use log2 for bits, natural log for nats.

short_description: A one-parameter generalization of Shannon entropy; includes collision and min-entropy.
assumptions: alpha > 0, alpha != 1
when_to_use: When you need a tunable measure of uncertainty (e.g. worst-case guessing).
when_not_to_use: When the standard additive Shannon entropy suffices.
special_cases: alpha->1 Shannon; alpha=2 collision entropy; alpha->inf min-entropy

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: At alpha=1 fall back to Shannon entropy.

short_description: A non-extensive generalization of Shannon entropy for systems with long-range correlations.
assumptions: alpha != 1
when_to_use: Systems where entropy is non-additive across subsystems.
when_not_to_use: Standard information-theoretic settings (use Shannon).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: T_2 = 1 - sum p_i^2 (Gini-Simpson index).

short_description: A two-parameter entropy that unifies Shannon, Renyi and Tsallis as limiting cases.
assumptions: alpha != 1, beta != 1
when_to_use: When you want one family that contains Shannon/Renyi/Tsallis.
when_not_to_use: When a single named entropy suffices.
special_cases: beta->alpha Renyi; beta->1 Tsallis; both ->1 Shannon

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Handle the alpha=1 and beta=1 limits separately.

short_description: The quantum analogue of Shannon entropy for a density matrix rho.
formula_plaintext: S(rho) = - tr(rho log rho) = - sum_i lambda_i log lambda_i
assumptions: rho is a density matrix (SPD, trace 1)
range: [0, log d] for a d-dimensional system
when_to_use: Quantifying uncertainty of a quantum state / density matrix.
when_not_to_use: Classical scalar distributions (use Shannon entropy).
special_cases: Shannon entropy of the eigenvalues of rho

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Compute on eigenvalues of rho.

short_description: The greatest distance from a point in one set to the nearest point in the other; a metric on compact sets.
formula_plaintext: dHaus(X,Y) = max( sup_x dist(x,Y), sup_y dist(X,y) )
assumptions: X, Y are subsets of a common metric space with ground distance rho
when_to_use: Comparing two point sets / shapes embedded in the same space.
when_not_to_use: When sets live in different spaces (use Gromov-Hausdorff).
special_cases: Directed Hausdorff distance keeps only one of the two sups

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Sensitive to outliers; consider quantile (modified) Hausdorff.

short_description: Distance between whole metric spaces up to isometry; measures how nearly two spaces can be aligned.
formula_plaintext: dGH(X,Y) = inf over isometric embeddings into Z of Hausdorff(phi_X(X), phi_Y(Y))
assumptions: X, Y are (compact) metric spaces
when_to_use: Comparing intrinsic geometry of shapes/graphs invariant to isometry.
when_not_to_use: When exact values are needed cheaply (computing it exactly is NP-hard).
special_cases: Two isometric shapes give d_GH = 0; usually approximated via Gromov-Wasserstein

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: NP-hard; approximate with the Gromov-Wasserstein relaxation (e.g. POT).
worked_example: Exact computation is NP-hard; isometric shapes give $d_{GH}=\mathbf{0}$.

short_description: The minimum cost to morph one distribution into another under a ground distance; a metric that respects geometry. EMD = Wasserstein-1.
formula_plaintext: W_alpha(p,q) = ( inf over couplings gamma of integral rho(x,y)^alpha dgamma )^(1/alpha)
assumptions: a ground distance rho on the sample space
for W_1 the Kantorovich-Rubinstein dual uses 1-Lipschitz test functions
when_to_use: When the ground geometry between outcomes matters and supports may not overlap.
when_not_to_use: When you only have densities on identical support and cheap divergences suffice; full OT is costly in high dimension.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: 1-D W_1 has a closed form (sort and integrate CDF gaps)
For higher dimensions use a solver such as POT; entropic Sinkhorn is a fast approximation

short_description: An IPM over an RKHS ball; the distance between kernel mean embeddings of two distributions. Estimable from samples.
assumptions: a characteristic kernel k (e.g. RBF) for MMD to be a metric
when_to_use: When you only have samples and want a differentiable, sample-estimable distance.
when_not_to_use: When kernel/bandwidth choice is unclear or ground transport geometry is essential.
special_cases: IPM with function class = unit RKHS ball

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Use the unbiased U-statistic estimator; tune kernel bandwidth (median heuristic).

short_description: An IPM built from a Stein operator; needs the score of the model but not its normalizing constant.
formula_plaintext: sup over Stein-RKHS ball of | E_q[ A_p f ] |, A_p the Stein operator using grad log p
assumptions: access to the score grad log p (unnormalized density is enough)
when_to_use: When the target is known only up to a normalizing constant and you have samples.
when_not_to_use: When the score is unavailable.
special_cases: Kernel Stein discrepancy uses an RKHS function class

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: No single closed-form number; estimate from samples and the model score.
worked_example: A supremum over a Stein-RKHS ball; in practice estimated from samples and the model score.

short_description: The largest gap between two cumulative distribution functions; the statistic of the KS goodness-of-fit test.
assumptions: one-dimensional distributions with CDFs F_p, F_q
when_to_use: Comparing 1-D distributions from samples without assuming a parametric form.
when_not_to_use: Multivariate data (KS is essentially one-dimensional).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Compare sorted empirical CDFs; scipy.stats.ks_2samp gives a p-value.

short_description: A metric that metrizes weak convergence of probability measures (convergence in distribution).
formula_plaintext: LP(p,q) = inf{ eps>0 : p(A) <= q(A^eps) + eps for all Borel A }
assumptions: a separable metric space with ground distance rho
when_to_use: When you need the canonical metric for convergence in distribution.
when_not_to_use: For routine computation (it is rarely evaluated directly).
special_cases: For a point mass shifted by delta, LP = min(delta, 1)

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Infimum over all Borel sets; no one-line value in general.
worked_example: For a point mass shifted by $\delta=0.3$: $\mathrm{LP}=\mathbf{0.3}$.

short_description: The geodesic distance on the manifold of SPD matrices; invariant to congruence transforms. A true metric.
formula_latex: d_{AI}(\mathbf P,\mathbf Q)=\big\|\log(\mathbf P^{-1/2}\mathbf Q\,\mathbf P^{-1/2})\big\|_F=\sqrt{\textstyle\sum_i \log^2\lambda_i}
formula_plaintext: dAI(P,Q) = || log(P^{-1/2} Q P^{-1/2}) ||_F = sqrt(sum_i log^2 lambda_i(P^{-1} Q))
assumptions: P, Q are SPD matrices
when_to_use: When you want a symmetric, congruence-invariant metric on covariance matrices.
when_not_to_use: When a cheap baseline suffices (Frobenius) or you need optimal-transport geometry (Bures-Wasserstein).
special_cases: Equals sqrt(sum log^2 of generalized eigenvalues of (P,Q))

references.title: Pennec et al., A Riemannian framework for tensor computing
Taxonomy (this site)

implementation_notes: Compute generalized eigenvalues of (P,Q) via Cholesky for stability; both inputs must be SPD.

short_description: The optimal-transport (Wasserstein-2) distance between zero-mean Gaussians with given covariances; a metric on SPD matrices.
formula_latex: d_{BW}^2(\mathbf P,\mathbf Q)=\mathrm{tr}\,\mathbf P+\mathrm{tr}\,\mathbf Q-2\,\mathrm{tr}\big((\mathbf P^{1/2}\mathbf Q\,\mathbf P^{1/2})^{1/2}\big)
assumptions: P, Q are SPD (covariance) matrices
when_to_use: When you want OT geometry between covariance matrices with a closed form.
when_not_to_use: When a congruence-invariant metric is preferred (affine-invariant Riemannian).
special_cases: The W_2 distance between N(0,P) and N(0,Q)

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Compute matrix square roots via eigendecomposition; clip tiny negative eigenvalues from round-off.

short_description: The largest singular value of the matrix difference; the operator (2-norm) distance between matrices.
formula_latex: d_2(\mathbf X,\mathbf Y)=\|\mathbf X-\mathbf Y\|_2=\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf X-\mathbf Y)
assumptions: X, Y are matrices of the same shape
when_to_use: When the largest directional deviation between matrices matters.
when_not_to_use: When you want aggregate (Frobenius) error or SPD-manifold geometry.
special_cases: For symmetric matrices equals the largest absolute eigenvalue of X-Y

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: np.linalg.norm(X-Y, 2).

short_description: Expected code length of q under the true distribution p; equals entropy plus KL divergence, and is the standard classification loss.
assumptions: p and q are probability distributions on the same support

identities.note: cross-entropy of p with itself is its entropy

when_to_use: As a training loss when q models the true labels p; minimizing it minimizes KL(p||q).
when_not_to_use: As a symmetric distance (it is asymmetric and nonzero at p=q; use KL or JS).
special_cases: Minimizing cross-entropy over q equals minimizing KL(p||q)

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Clip q with eps for the log
Renormalize inputs to sum to 1

short_description: One minus the cosine of the angle between two vectors; scale-invariant, ignores magnitude.
assumptions: x, y are nonzero real vectors
when_to_use: Comparing direction of high-dimensional vectors (embeddings) where magnitude is irrelevant.
when_not_to_use: When magnitude matters, or when you need a true metric (use angular distance / Euclidean).
special_cases: Angular distance arccos(1−d_cos) is a true metric

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Add eps to norms to avoid division by zero.

short_description: Sum of squared nearest-neighbour distances between two point clouds, in both directions; fast but not a metric.
assumptions: X, Y are finite point sets in a common space
when_to_use: Fast, differentiable comparison of point clouds when exact matching is too costly.
when_not_to_use: When you need the triangle inequality or robustness to outliers (use Hausdorff/Wasserstein).
special_cases: A one-sided version keeps a single direction

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: O(|X||Y|) with pairwise distances; use KD-trees for large clouds.

short_description: A distance between distributions from expected pairwise Euclidean distances; a special case of MMD, estimable from samples.
assumptions: finite first moments; X,X'~p and Y,Y'~q independent
when_to_use: Sample-based, kernel-free distribution comparison with a metric.
when_not_to_use: When ground transport geometry is essential (use Wasserstein).
special_cases: Distance-covariance / energy statistics build on the same idea

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Use the unbiased V-/U-statistic; O(n²) pairwise distances.

short_description: Entropy-regularized optimal transport, debiased so it vanishes at p=q; fast (Sinkhorn iterations) and differentiable.
formula_plaintext: min_gamma <gamma,C> + eps * KL(gamma || p x q)
assumptions: a ground cost C; regularization strength eps > 0
when_to_use: When exact Wasserstein is too costly; want a smooth, differentiable OT surrogate.
when_not_to_use: When you need the exact metric (small eps) or eps is hard to choose.
special_cases: eps→0 gives Wasserstein; large eps → an energy-distance-like MMD

references.title: POT: Python Optimal Transport
Taxonomy (this site)

implementation_notes: Compute with Sinkhorn iterations (e.g. POT ot.sinkhorn2 / ot.bregman).
worked_example: No one-line value; computed by Sinkhorn iterations (e.g. POT). eps→0 approaches the Wasserstein value.

short_description: Average of 1-D Wasserstein distances over random projections; a cheap, metric surrogate for Wasserstein.
assumptions: projections theta uniform on the sphere; 1-D W_p on each

identities.note: lower bounds full Wasserstein; equivalent in bounded domains

when_to_use: When full Wasserstein is too costly but you want a true metric that respects geometry.
when_not_to_use: When the projection count needed for accuracy is prohibitive in very high dimension.

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Average 1-D Wasserstein over many random unit directions.
worked_example: Estimated by averaging 1-D $W_1$ over random projections; more projections → lower variance.

short_description: Optimal transport between metric-measure spaces: matches pairwise distances rather than points, so it compares spaces up to isometry.
assumptions: metric-measure spaces with intra-space distances d_X, d_Y
when_to_use: Comparing objects in different spaces (no common embedding), invariant to isometry.
when_not_to_use: When a shared space exists (plain Wasserstein is cheaper).

references.title: POT: Python Optimal Transport
Taxonomy (this site)

implementation_notes: Non-convex; approximate with POT ot.gromov (entropic GW).
worked_example: No closed form; approximated (e.g. POT). Two isometric spaces give GW = 0.

short_description: Unbalanced optimal transport that both moves and creates/destroys mass; interpolates Wasserstein (transport) and Fisher-Rao (growth).
formula_plaintext: dynamic unbalanced OT: transport cost |v|^2 + growth cost delta^2 alpha^2
assumptions: nonnegative measures of possibly different mass; scale delta
when_to_use: When distributions have different total mass or local creation/destruction matters.
when_not_to_use: When mass is conserved (plain Wasserstein suffices).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Solve via unbalanced-OT solvers (e.g. POT ot.unbalanced).
worked_example: No one-line value; computed by unbalanced-OT solvers. Limits give W₂ or Fisher–Rao.

short_description: A similarity between two representations via their centered Gram matrices; invariant to orthogonal transforms and isotropic scaling. Distance = 1 − CKA.
assumptions: K, L are Gram matrices of two representations of the same n samples
when_to_use: Comparing learned representations regardless of rotation/scaling of features.
when_not_to_use: As a metric between distributions (it compares representation geometry).
special_cases: Linear CKA uses linear kernels K=XXᵀ; RBF CKA uses RBF Gram matrices

references.title: Kornblith et al., Similarity of Neural Network Representations Revisited

implementation_notes: Center Gram matrices with H = I − 1/n; report 1 − CKA as a distance.
worked_example: Identical representations give CKA = 1 (distance 0); unrelated ones ≈ 0.

short_description: The Jensen gap of the Rényi entropy; a bounded, symmetric divergence generalizing Jensen-Shannon (which is the α→1 case).
assumptions: p, q probability distributions; H_alpha is the Rényi entropy
when_to_use: When you want a tunable, bounded, symmetric divergence between distributions.
when_not_to_use: When a single fixed divergence suffices (use JS).

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Compute Rényi entropies of p, q and their mean; subtract.

short_description: Minimum-cost sequence of node/edge insertions, deletions and substitutions transforming one graph into another; a metric, but NP-hard.
formula_plaintext: min over edit paths of sum of edit costs c(e_i)
assumptions: edit cost function c over node/edge insert/delete/substitute
when_to_use: Structural comparison of small graphs where edit semantics matter.
when_not_to_use: Large graphs (NP-hard) — use spectral or approximate GED.
special_cases: A graph analogue of Levenshtein edit distance

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: NP-hard exactly; use approximations (bipartite/ Hausdorff-GED) e.g. networkx.graph_edit_distance.
worked_example: Two identical graphs give GED = 0; one edge insertion at unit cost gives GED = 1.

short_description: Distance between graphs from the difference of their sorted Laplacian eigenvalues; fast and permutation-invariant.
formula_plaintext: sqrt( sum_i (lambda_i^(1) - lambda_i^(2))^2 ), sorted Laplacian spectra
assumptions: graphs compared via (possibly padded) sorted Laplacian eigenvalues
when_to_use: Fast, permutation-invariant graph comparison when structure ≈ captured by the spectrum.
when_not_to_use: When cospectral (spectrum-equal but non-isomorphic) graphs must be distinguished.
special_cases: Can use the adjacency or normalized Laplacian spectrum instead

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Pad the shorter spectrum with zeros; compare sorted eigenvalues.
worked_example: Isomorphic graphs give 0 (equal spectra); differing degree structure gives a positive value.

short_description: Residual Frobenius distance between two point sets after the best rotation (and optionally scaling/translation) aligns them.
formula_plaintext: min over orthogonal R of ||X - Y R||_F
assumptions: X, Y are n×d configurations in correspondence
when_to_use: Comparing shapes/configurations invariant to rotation (and optionally scale/translation).
when_not_to_use: When point correspondence is unknown (use Chamfer/Wasserstein first).
special_cases: Full Procrustes also optimizes scaling and translation

references.title: Taxonomy of Principal Distances & Divergences (this site)

implementation_notes: Optimal R from the SVD of XᵀY (orthogonal Procrustes).
worked_example: If Y is X rotated, the optimal R undoes the rotation and $d_{\mathrm{Proc}}=0$.
